title: Context from the Future
concept: In 'The ___ bank of the river', the word 'river' at the end reveals that 'bank' means land, not a finance building — future context is essential for homonyms.

exercises.prompt: Why can words like 'bank' be confusing when you first read them?

options.text: They can mean more than one thing depending on context.
They can only be used at the very start of a sentence.

exercises.explain: Words like 'bank' or 'bat' that are spelled the same but have different meanings are called homonyms. Context clues help us tell them apart!

cards.title: Reading into the Future
cards.body: Have you ever started reading a sentence and had to change your mind about what a word meant halfway through? Sometimes, the true meaning of a word is a secret that only the future can reveal!

cards.title: What is Future Context?
cards.body: Future context is information that comes later in a sentence. When we read a word with multiple meanings (like 'bank'), we often have to wait for the upcoming words to figure out which meaning is the right one.

cards.title: The Detective Novel
cards.body: Imagine reading a detective novel. You see a shadow in a window, but you don't know who it is. Only when you read the next chapter do you finally understand what that shadow meant. Similarly, your brain holds onto a mysterious word like 'bank' and waits for the rest of the sentence to reveal its true identity!

cards.title: Waiting for the River
cards.body: Look at this sentence: 'He deposited his money at the bank.' The word 'money' comes earlier and helps us. But in: 'We walked along the bank of the muddy river,' we only know 'bank' means land once we reach the very end of the sentence!

cards.title: The Instant Understanding Myth
cards.body: You might think we read and understand every single word instantly, one by one. In reality, our brains often hold words in a 'waiting room' until future words arrive to clarify what they mean!

cards.title: One Word, Two Futures
cards.body: Depending on the words that follow, the exact same word can mean two completely different things. We must wait for the future words to know which is which!

right.text: 'The bank... was covered in green moss.'

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'She picked up the bow and played her violin,' how do we know what 'bow' means?

options.text: A wooden tool used to play a stringed instrument

exercises.explain: Until you read 'violin', the word 'bow' could mean a ribbon, a knot, or bending forward. The future word 'violin' clarifies it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Future context means looking at the words that came BEFORE the tricky word.
exercises.explain: Future context means we look ahead, not backward, to find the clues we need to understand a word.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the concept of waiting for clues.
exercises.sentence: To understand some words, we must look forward to the ___ context.
exercises.explain: Words that come later in a sentence provide future context, helping us decode words with double meanings.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses future context to clarify that 'bat' means an animal, rather than sports equipment?

options.text: The fly flew out of the open window.
The bat flew directly back into its dark cave.
He hit the baseball with a wooden bat.

exercises.explain: The sentence 'The fly flew away' uses 'fly' as a noun first, but it doesn't wait until the very end to clarify it like the river example does. The second option explicitly shows how 'bat' is a mystery until we hit the future word 'cave'.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'She sat on the bank of the river', why is the word 'river' so important?

options.text: It tells us how deep the river is.
It provides the future context needed to know 'bank' means land, not a place for money.
It makes the sentence longer and harder to read.
It changes the spelling of the word 'bank'.

exercises.explain: Without the word 'river' at the end, the word 'bank' remains a mystery. The future context is what finally unlocks the correct meaning!
